title: UX Discovery: Product Development Process
subtitle: Understanding where research fits in our org
description: Goal: understand where the current process breaks down before pitching any solution. Do NOT mention InsightCatcher or any specific tool. Listen for pain, don't create it.

rules: I've been looking at how we validate new product ideas and I'd love to understand how it actually works in practice today — before I suggest anything.
There are no right or wrong answers — I'm trying to learn, not audit.
I may stay quiet at times — I just want to make sure I hear the full picture.
setup.noHelpPolicy: Do NOT mention InsightCatcher, research tools, or any solution you have in mind. If they ask what you're building, say 'I'm still trying to understand the problem first.' This is a learning session, not a pitch. If they ask leading questions back at you, deflect and return to their experience.

warmup.intro: Start with context-setting before diving into specifics. You want them talking about their world, not reacting to your agenda.
warmup.why: These questions establish their frame of reference — how they think about validation, what success looks like to them. Everything else will be filtered through this.

questions.label: Set the scene
questions.question: Can you walk me through the last product idea that went through the full process — from pitch to validation? What did that actually look like in practice?
questions.listenFor: Where do they slow down or hedge? What steps feel solid vs. hand-wavy? Are they describing a structured process or a series of gut calls?

questions.label: What good looks like
questions.question: When a product idea has gone really well — where the team felt confident they were building the right thing — what made that different?
questions.listenFor: Do they cite user evidence, sales feedback, SME intuition, or data? This tells you what they currently count as validation.

tasks.name: The idea pitch and what's behind it
tasks.subtitle: Understanding evidence quality at the pitch stage
tasks.script: When an idea pitch comes to the review board — what evidence is usually attached to it? What does that typically look like in practice?
tasks.success: You understand what 'customer feedback' means to them concretely — is it a sales call recap, a client email, structured interviews, or something else. You know whether evidence is required or just expected.
watchFor: Vague references to 'client conversations' without specifics
Sales or account teams as the primary source of user feedback
'We usually just know' or SME intuition used as evidence
No defined format for what evidence should look like
Difference between what the process says and what actually happens
tasks.learnWhy: If evidence at the pitch stage is thin or informal, that's the highest-leverage intervention point. Everything downstream gets built on that foundation.

tasks.name: When pitches fail or get sent back
tasks.subtitle: Finding where the process breaks down
tasks.script: When a pitch has been rejected or sent back — what was usually missing? And has there been a product that got greenlit and then struggled — where in hindsight the team wished they'd known something earlier?
tasks.success: You have a concrete example of a gap between what was believed and what turned out to be true. Ideally with a cost attached — time, money, rework.
watchFor: Any mention of 'we found out during build' or 'EAP surprised us'
Features built that users didn't adopt or understand
Assumptions about users that turned out to be wrong
Buyer feedback mistaken for user feedback
Reluctance to name a specific example — may mean the pain is there but politically sensitive
tasks.learnWhy: Concrete past failures are the clearest signal that a problem is real and costly. This is your strongest evidence that structured discovery has value here.

note.title: If they can't name an example
note.content: Don't push. Ask: 'Is there a type of feedback you wish you had more of when ideas are being evaluated?' That's a softer version of the same question.

tasks.name: How the team talks to users today
tasks.subtitle: Understanding current research habits
tasks.script: When PMs or SMEs talk to clients during the idea or validation phase — what does that usually look like? Is it structured, or more of a conversation? And what typically gets captured from those conversations — where does it go?
tasks.success: You know who talks to users, how often, in what format, and what happens to the output. You know whether 'talking to clients' means economic buyers or actual end users.
watchFor: Conflation of buyer and end user — 'our clients love it' vs 'the analysts who use it'
Notes living in someone's inbox or head, not shared or structured
SMEs acting as proxies for users rather than direct user access
No consistent format — everyone does it differently
Access to real end users being blocked by account management or client relationships
tasks.learnWhy: This reveals the actual current state of discovery — not the process diagram version. If conversations happen but produce nothing shareable, that's the gap.

tasks.name: The EAP and feedback cycles
tasks.subtitle: Is feedback structured or informal?
tasks.script: During EAP — what does 'rapid feedback cycles' actually look like right now? How structured is it, and when feedback comes back, how does it turn into product changes?
tasks.success: You understand whether EAP feedback is structured (tasks, observations, findings doc) or informal (call notes, account manager relay). You know whether changes are still feasible at that point.
watchFor: Feedback going through implementation services rather than directly to product
'By then it's hard to change things' — signals EAP is too late for meaningful iteration
No defined format for what feedback should look like
Confusion about who owns the feedback loop
EAP being treated as validation for launch, not learning for improvement
tasks.learnWhy: EAP is the most natural place to introduce structured usability testing — it's already in the process. If it's currently informal, there's an easy win here.

tasks.name: The most expensive mistake
tasks.subtitle: Surfacing real pain
tasks.script: What's the most expensive mistake the team has made recently from building something that turned out not to work the way users expected? And — if you could know one thing earlier in the process than you currently do, what would it be?
tasks.success: You have a concrete example with real cost attached. You know what information they wish they had. This is your clearest signal of where structured research would have made a difference.
watchFor: Any story about late-stage discovery of a fundamental problem
Features that were built but not adopted
Users who 'workaround' the product rather than using it as designed
Rework that happened after EAP or even after launch
The answer to 'what would you want to know earlier' — this is your brief
tasks.learnWhy: This is the Mom's Test equivalent of 'what are the implications of that?' — separating real, costly pain from mild inconvenience. If they have a vivid answer, the problem is real.

note.title: This is the most important task
note.content: Don't rush past a good answer here. If they give you a real example, ask follow-up probes: 'When did you first realize something was off?' / 'What would have needed to be true earlier to avoid that?' / 'How much time or money did that cost roughly?'

tasks.name: Constraints and feasibility
tasks.subtitle: Understanding what's actually possible
tasks.script: When PMs are in the idea or validation phase — how much time realistically do they have for research activities? And is access to actual end users straightforward, or does that go through account management?
tasks.success: You understand the real constraints: time, access to users, org politics around client relationships. These determine what solutions are actually viable.
watchFor: Access to end users requiring account manager approval or client permission
PMs having very little unscheduled time in validation phase
'We'd have to be careful about that with clients' — signals relationship sensitivity
A distinction between EAP clients (open access) and general clients (restricted)
Any mention of internal users or analysts who could be accessed more easily
tasks.learnWhy: A great solution that requires access you don't have is not a solution. Knowing the constraints shapes what you propose.

wrapup.intro: Close with open questions that surface what they value and what they'd welcome. This is the only moment where you can hint at your agenda — gently.

questions.label: What's working
questions.question: Is there anything about how we currently validate ideas that you feel is working well — something we should be careful not to disrupt?

questions.label: The one thing
questions.question: If UX could make one thing easier for the team in the early phases of a new product — before the design work starts — what would be most valuable?

questions.label: Open door
questions.question: Is there anyone else on the team you think I should talk to about this — maybe someone who's felt the friction most directly?

observerNotes.behavior: Names a specific product failure or late discovery
observerNotes.meaning: Real, felt pain — strong signal that structured research would have value. Note the example carefully.

observerNotes.behavior: Says 'we talk to clients' but can't describe the format
observerNotes.meaning: Research is happening but unstructured — good entry point for lightweight tooling

observerNotes.behavior: Conflates buyer with end user throughout
observerNotes.meaning: Low UX maturity signal — education needed before tooling. Start with the distinction, not the tool.

observerNotes.behavior: Says 'by EAP it's hard to change things'
observerNotes.meaning: Pain exists at a specific point — EAP structured testing is your clearest quick win

observerNotes.behavior: Mentions access to users going through account management
observerNotes.meaning: Real constraint — solution must work within existing client relationships, not require new access

observerNotes.behavior: Can't answer 'what would you want to know earlier'
observerNotes.meaning: Either pain isn't acute enough, or they haven't connected the dots yet. May need more time before proposing anything.

observerNotes.behavior: Enthusiastic about the last wrapup question
observerNotes.meaning: They've been thinking about this already — there's appetite. Now is the time to follow up with something concrete.

observerNotes.behavior: Refers you to a PM or SME for more detail
observerNotes.meaning: Good sign — they're engaged enough to help you learn more. Follow the thread.
